coredata.dc:title: Simple method for fabrication of metal-organic framework within a carboxymethylcellulose/graphene quantum dots matrix as a carrier for anticancer drug
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
Salehi, Roya
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible drug delivery vehicles with sustained drug release property are valuable in cancer therapy and can reduce some of the side effects. Hence, to achieve the biocompatible system with sustained drug release behavior a new drug carrier was fabricated via in situ synthesis of MIL-53 (MIL = Materials of Institute Lavoisier) within the carboxymethylcellulose/graphene quantum dots matrix (CMC/GQDs) as a biological macromolecule based platform (MIL-53@CMC/GQDs). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed successful synthesis of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The mean pore diameter of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs obtained 18.66 nm. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) exhibited that MIL-53 is well distributed in hydrogel matrix. Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded about 55.80% and 88.90% into the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, respectively. Drug release studies showed the pH-dependent DOX release behavior for DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The cytotoxic assay approved the biocompatibility of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs against the human breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB 231). The fragmentation of nuclei and condensation of chromatin after treatment with DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed its capability in cancer treatment. Moreover, an arrest in sub-G1 of cell cycle after treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs showed cell's apoptosis. The results conveyed a new concept that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs could be proposed as a potential carrier for the delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-nanocomposite
Carboxymethylcellulose
Breast cancer
Drug delivery
Doxorubicin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix (MIL-53@CMC/GQDs)
In vitro DOX load and release study
Observation of the nuclear change by using DAPI staining

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by a research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/2708-980701 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra for the GQDs, CMC, CMC/GQDs, MIL-53, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite.
XRD patterns of GQDs, CMC/GQDs, MIL-53, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite (a), EDX spectrum of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (b), and the cross-section of the sample with EDX mapping images (c).
The SEM images of MIL-53, CMC/GQDs, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite in two different magnifications.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (a), PL spectra of the GQDs, CMC/GQDs, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs after excitation at 365 nm (b).
DOX loading percentage for MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (a), in vitro DOX release behavior for DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs at 37 °C and two different pH (pH 7.4 and pH 5.0) (b).
Viability of MDA-MB 231 cells after 48h exposure to MIL-53, MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (a), and DOX, DOX@MIL-53, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (b). The results were considered as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

Fluorescence microscopy images of the DAPI stained MDA-MB 231 cells after treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite (b), DOX (c), and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (d). The non treated cells considered as control (a).
Cell cycle distributions for MDA-MB 231 cells after treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (b), DOX (c), and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (d). The non treated cells considered as control (a).
The schematic route for the preparation of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite.
Mathematical models correlation coefficients for DOX release from the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Simple method for fabrication of metal-organic framework within a carboxymethylcellulose/graphene quantum dots matrix as a carrier for anticancer drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Natural Polymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Natural Polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Natural polymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Department of Advanced Medical Science, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Applied Research Center, and Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Department of Advanced Medical Science, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Natural Polymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Natural Polymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatible drug delivery vehicles with sustained drug release property are valuable in cancer therapy and can reduce some of the side effects. Hence, to achieve the biocompatible system with sustained drug release behavior a new drug carrier was fabricated via in situ synthesis of MIL-53 (MIL = Materials of Institute Lavoisier) within the carboxymethylcellulose/graphene quantum dots matrix (CMC/GQDs) as a biological macromolecule based platform (MIL-53@CMC/GQDs). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed successful synthesis of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The mean pore diameter of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs obtained 18.66 nm. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) exhibited that MIL-53 is well distributed in hydrogel matrix. Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded about 55.80% and 88.90% into the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, respectively. Drug release studies showed the pH-dependent DOX release behavior for DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The cytotoxic assay approved the biocompatibility of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs against the human breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB 231). The fragmentation of nuclei and condensation of chromatin after treatment with DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed its capability in cancer treatment. Moreover, an arrest in sub-G1 of cell cycle after treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs showed cell's apoptosis. The results conveyed a new concept that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs could be proposed as a potential carrier for the delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years the drug delivery systems with burst drug release behavior became less important and more research focused on developing the new systems with controlled and sustained drug release ability [1–7]. Up to now, the various systems have been introduced as drug delivery systems with controlled drug release behavior, among them, hydrogels are the widely investigated systems due to their special properties [8–13]. So, the systems based on the hydrogels were evaluated as a carrier for several curing agents like Vitamin C, doxorubicin (DOX), green tea polyphenols, etc. [14–17]. Hydrogels are the cross-linked polymers with a three-dimensional structure and more hydrophilic functional groups. Crosslinking of the polymers could be done through physical or chemical crosslinking methods [18–22]. In comparison to the physically cross-linked hydrogels, the use of organic molecules as a chemical crosslinker leads to the hydrogels with more strength. The swelling without dissolving in aqueous media, biocompatibility, and high hydrophilicity resulted in the suitability of the hydrogels for use in the biomedical areas; especially in the drug delivery field [23–25]. Both natural or synthetic polymers could be crosslinked to form the hydrogels [18,26–29]. Because of the low cost, biocompatibility, pH sensitivity, and degradation capability, the carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) based hydrogels have been used widely in numerous areas like drug delivery applications [18,30–34]. This biopolymer as a main anionic derivative of the cellulose is synthesized via the introduction of the -CH2COOH groups in the cellulose chains. In comparison with pristine hydrogels, nanocomposite hydrogels are more suitable as drug delivery vehicles due to their improved properties. These materials have the advantages of both hydrogel and nanoparticles (NPs). Various types of NPs have been used for the fabrication of hydrogel nanocomposites which metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), graphene and its derivatives, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanotubes, magnetite, and layered double hydroxides (LDHs), are some of them [27,35–39].

MOFs are the crystalline coordination polymers with a porous structure and made through the formation of the powerful bridging bonds between the metal ions and organic ligands. In recent years, MOFs have been attracted considerable attention in biomedicine owing to narrow pore size distributions, various chemical reactive sites, and their large surface areas [40–44]. Usually, the MOFs are prepared in the medium with high temperature and pressure through the solvothermal method. Hence, the development of the preparation method that could perform in a short time and at room is required. As well as, the use of water as a cheap and safe solvent makes it as an ideal method. MIL-53 (MIL = Materials of Institute Lavoisier) is one member of the MOF family that could be used in a wide area due to its stability and porous structure. Although the blending method is the commonly used way for the preparation of the MOF/polymer composites it has two main disadvantages; 1) Poor interaction between polymer matrix and particles, and 2) Occurring the particle aggregation during the preparation step. These limitations could be solved via the in situ synthesis of MOFs within the polymer matrix [41]. Hence, in this work, we synthesized the MIL-53 within a CMC/GQDs matrix via a simple in situ method.

Graphene as a conjugated macromolecule is a two-dimensional sheet comprising sp2 bonded carbon atoms with a large specific area [45–47]. GQDs are a member of the graphene family with tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, strong quantum confinement, and good biocompatibility. In comparison with graphite oxide, the GQDs have a higher water-solubility, larger surface area, and more functional groups. The special features of the GQDs could provide the possibility of drug loading through the possible π–π stacking between aromatic rings of drugs and aromatic regions of the GQDs, as well as, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions between the polar functional groups of carriers with hydroxyl and carboxyl functional of GQDs [48–52]. The composition of CMC with GQDs could upgrade the drug loading capacity [18], as well as, increase the number of carboxylic groups in the structure of hydrogel that are the appropriate sits for MOF growth.

By considering cancer as one of the main reasons for human death, and as part of our ongoing program, related to the developing of new biocompatible drug delivery systems for cancer treatment [7,18,53], and based on our previous investigations on CMC [54,55] and GQDs [19,56], in current work, for the first time, we have introduced a new green, room temperature, and a simple method for in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the biocompatible CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix as a template. So, to the best of our knowledge on the published papers to this day, there was not any report to show the preparation of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and evaluation its capability as DOX carrier. Therefore, the prepared DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs evaluated as a novel carrier for DOX delivery. Several analysis approved the success in the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs preparation. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) maps showed the good distribution of MIL-53 in the CMC/GQDs matrix. MIL-53@CMC/GQDs exhibited improved drug loading and biocompatibility in comparison with neat MIL-53. The DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed the controlled release behavior for DOX. The 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) staining, and cell cycle analysis displayed a high amount of apoptosis on the treated MDA-MB 231cancer cells (Human breast cancer cell line) with the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. Based on the obtained results the prepared system potentially could be proposed as a hopeful carrier for biomedical applications.

CMC with viscosity about 15,000 mPas/s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) and degree of substitution of 0.55–1.0 was provided from Nippon Paper Industries (Japan). Citric acid (CA), sodium hydroxide, epichlorohydrin (ECH), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3.9H2O), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were supplied from Merck. Disodium terephthalate (Na2BDC), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), propidium iodide (PI), and DAPI staining dyes were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. DOX obtained from the Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. The double-distilled water utilized in all steps of the work. Roswell park memorial institute 1640 growth medium (RPMI), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, and other used materials for the biological tests were provided from Gibco, USA. Phosphate-buffered saline (abbreviated PBS) and acetate buffer were prepared according to the common laboratory methods and used for the following experiments. The MDA-MB-231 cells prepared from the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra recorded in the 4000–400 cm−1 and a resolution of 0.5 cm−1 by using the Bruker Instruments (model Aquinox 55, Germany) as KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements in the scan range of 2θ from 5° to 70° were performed by using the Siemens diffractometer at room temperature and Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV. The EDX and SEM analysis were obtained using the TESCANMIRA3 electron microscope operating at 30 kV. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra achieved using the Shimadzu 1700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm was investigated by using a gas adsorption analyzer (BELsorp, Japan) at −196°C. The PL spectra recorded by using the LS45, PerkinElmer spectrofluorometer. The PHS-3C pH meter (A digital pH meter) was used for adjustment of the pHs.

MIL-53 was synthesized according to the reference [57] with some modifications. In this regard, at first, an aqueous solution of Al(NO3)3.9H2O (0.6 g, 1.6 mmol) was prepared. In the following, about 8 mL of Na2BDC aqueous solution (0.168 g, 0.8 mmol) slowly was added to the previously prepared Al-containing solution under the magnetic stirring. With gradually increasing the Na2BDC concentration in the reaction medium, the white precipitates were formed. The stirring of the mixture continued overnight. The obtained precipitate was separated from the solution using the centrifuging and washed three times with water and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C.

GQDs were synthesized from the direct pyrolysis of CA according to [58] with little changes. In general, 2.0 g of CA was poured in a pyrex beaker and then placed in the heating mantle with 200 °C. After 30 min the orange liquid was obtained. Under vigorous stirring, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into the aqueous solution of NaOH (10 mg/mL) and neutralized to pH 7.0. The obtained aqueous solution of GQDs was stored at 4 °C for later usages.

For the preparation of CMC/GQDs at first, about 1.0 g of CMC was dissolved in the NaOH solution (3%) containing 0.3 g of GQDs. Mechanical stirring led to the formation of the homogeneous viscous mixture. The ECH was added dropwise (within 2 h) to the reaction mixture. Afterward, the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 2 h. The obtained cross-linked CMC/GQDs paste washed several times with water to remove the excess of ECH and NaOH. In the end, the prepared CMC/GQDs dried into a vacuum oven at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix (MIL-53@CMC/GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: For the in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix, a definite amount of crushed CMC/GQDs hydrogel was poured into the 3 mL of the aqueous solution having Al(NO3)3.9H2O (0.1 g, 0.266 mmol). After 24 h stirring at room temperature, the mixture centrifuged and the product moved into the 3 mL of Na2BDC aqueous solution (0.028 g, 0.133 mmol). The mixture stirred for another 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the prepared MIL-53@CMC/GQDs separated from the solution by using the centrifuge. After washing with water for several times, the prepared MIL-53@CMC/GQDs was dried into a vacuum oven at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX load and release study
ce:section.ce:para:
For evaluation of the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs drug loading capacity, the sample separately were suspended in DOX solution (100 mL, 200 ppm). The mixture was shacked on a shaker at room temperature and in the dark condition. After 24 h, the drug-loaded systems removed from the solution through the centrifuging, and the supernatant was collected. For the elimination of the surface adsorbed DOX from the systems, they washed two times with a small amount of PBS and then dried in the vacuum oven. The collected supernatant was used for calculation of the DOX loading amount by using the UV–Vis spectroscopy at λ = 480 nm. Overall, the obtained absorption for the supernatant was translated to the drug concentration by using the calibration curve of DOX and the amount of the unloaded DOX was achieved (ppm). Multiplying of the DOX concentration to the volume achieved the unloaded DOX (mg). Subtracting the amount of the unloaded DOX (mg) from the initial amount of DOX (mg) obtained the amount of loaded DOX (mg). Finally, the drug loading capacity (%) and drug entrapment efficiency (%) were determined by using the Eq. 1 and Eq. 2, respectively.
(1)
DOX
loading capacity
%
=
Mass of
DOX
in carrier
Mass of carrier
× 100
(2)
DOX
entrapment efficiency
%
=
Mass of
DOX
in carrier
Mass of
DOX
fed
initially
× 100

Because the amount of DOX loading and cell viability for the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs was higher than neat MIL-53, we studied drug release profile, and other biological tests only for MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The DOX release profile was investigated by using the cumulative method at 37 °C and two different pHs 7.4 and 5.0. In this regard, DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs was immersed into 10 mL of buffer solutions. In the following, the sealed vials were shaken under dark conditions at 37 °C. At the definite time intervals, 3 mL of supernatant was sampled and the same amount of fresh buffer (3 mL) was replaced. The absorption of the sampled solution was obtained by using the UV–Vis spectroscopy. The obtained absorption was converted to the mg of the released drug (Similar to the drug loading process). In the end, the amount of the released DOX (percentage) was determined by using the Eq. (3).
(3)
Drug release
%
=
Amount of released drug
Amount of
lo a ded
drug
× 100

Four common types of mathematical models; zero-order (Eq. (4)), first-order (Eq. (5)), Higuchi (Eq. (6)), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (Eq. (7)) were applied to investigate the in vitro release kinetics of DOX from DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite. The related equation for each studied model is listed below.
(4)
Q =
k 0
t
(5)
LnQ = Ln
Q 0
−
k 1
t
(6)
Q =
k H
t
1 2
(7)
LnQ = nLnt + Ln
k p
where, k0, k1, kH, and kP respectively are the rate constants of the zero-order, first-order, Korsmeyer-Peppas, and Higuchi kinetic models. Moreover, the Q is the amount of the released DOX. For all of the used models, the correlation coefficient parameter (R2) was determined after fitting the cumulative drug release data with each equation. The mechanism of drug release was determined through the calculated ‘n’ diffusional exponent; n = 1, 0.45<n > 1, and n ≤ 0.45 respectively shows that the DOX release from the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs through a case-II transport, non-Fickian transport, and Fickian diffusion.

MDA-MB 231 cells selected as a model of cancer cells for the in vitro cellular analysis. In this regard, MDA-MB 231 cells were cultured in complete medium containing RPMI 1640, FBS (10% v/v), and Penstrep (1% v/v) and then incubated in the atmospheric and thermal conditions of 5% carbon dioxide (CO2), 95% humidity, and 37 °C.

The relative cytotoxicity of the drug, as well as, free and drug-loaded carrier evaluated against the MDA-MB 231 cells by using the MTT assay. In this regard, the cells were seeded in the 96-well plates at a seeding density of 104 cells per well. After 24 h incubation, the cultured cells in the 96-well plates treated with MIL-53, MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, DOX, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C. The untreated cells considered as control. After 48 h incubation, the unused RPMI removed and the MTT solution (200 μL, 5 mg/mL) added to each well. The cells incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. In the next step, the unreacted MTT was drained. DMSO (200 μL) was added to each well and the 96-well plates shaken for 15 min to dissolve the formed purple-blue MTT formazan crystals. The solution absorbance was determined at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm using the ELISA plate reader to calculate the cell viability. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs was evaluated against MCF10A (normal breast cells) as the above-mentioned procedure. The MTT test was performed in triplicate and the cell viability (%) was determined using the following equation:
(8)
Cell viability
%
=
Mean absorbance of each test
Mean absorbance of controls
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of the nuclear change by using DAPI staining
ce:section.ce:para: DAPI staining method was used for assessing the nucleus apoptosis after the treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, DOX, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. At first, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in six-well plates having sterile coverslips at a density of 250,000 cells per well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The attached cells treated with DOX and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs at their half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as, blank nanocarrier at definite concentration. The cells with no treatment considered as control. After 48 h incubation at 37 °C, the cells washed with fresh PBS and fixed with 3 wt% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature. Extra of the paraformaldehyde was removed through the rinsing of the cells with PBS. Subsequently, the cells permeabilized with Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v) for 15 min. Cells washed with PBS and then stained with DAPI for 5 min. A fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan) was used to observe the apoptotic nuclei (fragmented or wrinkled).

To study the effect of treating with samples on the cycle of the selected cells, MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in six-well plates with initial populations of 5 × 105. To reach the maximum density of cell/well (80% confluence), the MDA-MB 231 cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. In the following, the attached cells were treated with DOX and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs at their half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as, blank nanocarrier at definite concentration and the cells incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Non-treated cells were considered as control. After 48 h incubation, the cells washed with PBS, trypsinized, and collected. The collected cells fixed using the cold ethanol and then stored at 4 °C for 72 h. In the end, the cells were stained with the PI solution and RnaseA (Cinagen). Cells population in different cell cycle phases was determined by using the FACSCalibur (Becton Dickinson).

MIL-53 as a porous material was synthesized within the CMC/GQDs matrix to obtain the new drug delivery system with improved biocompatibility and drug loading capability. At first, a CMC/GQDs hydrogel was prepared as a template through the chemical crosslinking of the CMC biopolymer and GQDs by using the ECH as a crosslinker. The crushed CMC/GQDs were moved into the Al solution. After overnight stirring at room temperature, the Al ions were coordinated with the carboxyl groups of the CMC and GQDs. In the following, the hydrogel was placed into the Na2BDC aqueous solution. Stirring for the 24 h at room temperature led to the in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix. The efficiency of the prepared MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs as drug delivery systems was evaluated from the drug loading and biocompatibility viewpoint and compared with each other. Because of obtaining better results for MIL-53@CMC/GQDs the additional tests were performed on MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite. The schematic route for the preparation of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs is shown in Scheme 1 .

FT-IR analysis was used to study the successful synthesis of GQDs, CMC/GQDs, MIL-53, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The obtained spectra are shown in Fig. 1 . The GQDs displayed the main peaks at 3248 cm−1, 3031 cm−1, 1393 cm−1, and 1122 cm−1 which respectively are the characteristic peaks of the stretching vibration of the -OH groups, stretching vibration of the –CH, symmetric stretching vibrations of -COO−, and stretching vibration of C-O. Moreover, the GQDs exhibited a broad peak in 1600–1800 cm−1 that is due to the merging of the asymmetric stretching frequency of -COO− and stretching vibration of C=C [59,60]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the CMC, the observed broad peak at 3500 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of the -OH groups. Also, the appeared bands in 2924 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 1444 cm−1, and 1060 cm−1 respectively are related to the stretching vibration of the –CH2, stretching vibration of the carboxyl group of CMC, bending vibration of the -OH, and stretching vibration of CH-O-CH2. The FT-IR spectrum of the CMC/GQDs is a mathematic combination of the GQDs and CMC FT-IR spectra. This observation could be a good sign for the presence of GQDs in the network of the CMC/GQDs. For MIL-53 the observed peaks in 1580 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 are related to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the -COO−. The symmetric stretching vibration of -COO− appeared in 1421 cm−1. The appearance of the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the -COO− could be a good sign for the coordination of the Al ions to the carboxylate groups. Moreover, the observed peak at 3416 cm−1 of this spectrum is attributed to the entrapped water in the framework of MIL-53 [61]. All identified characteristic peaks of MIL-53 and CMC/GQDs were observed in the spectrum of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite which could be good evidence for successful in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix.

The XRD pattern of each material is the main criterion for the study of its crystalline structure. The XRD patterns of GQDs, CMC/GQDs, MIL-53, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite are displayed in Fig. 2a. As shown in Fig. 2a, the GQDs have the main wide peak at 2θ 24° which is related to its hexagonal lattice structure with (002) lattice spacing. By using the Bragg equation, the interlayer spacing obtained about 3.7 Å. The calculated value for the interlayer spacing of GQDs is similar to the obtained value for the graphitic materials and could support the formation of the GQDs from the pyrolysis of CA [58]. CMC/GQDs hydrogel displayed a broad peak at 2θ 18°-28° of its XRD spectrum that comes from the amorphous nature of the hydrogel structure. In the XRD spectrum of the synthesized MIL-53 the characteristic peaks observed at 2θ values of 8.49°, 8.89°, 16.4°, 17.43°, 20.6°, 25.26°, and 27.96° which appearance of them imply the successful synthesis of MIL-53. In the following, the XRD spectrum of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite was recorded. The presence of the MIL-53 XRD peaks in the XRD spectrum of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite could be good evidence for the in situ synthesis of the MIL-53 within MIL-53@CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix [57,62]. EDX analysis was used for further evaluation of the in situ synthesis of MIL-53 in the CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix. The obtained spectrum is illustrated in Fig. 2b. The presence of C, O, and Al elements in the EDX spectrum of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs is a good reason for the in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix. For more study about the distribution of the MIL-53 in the hydrogel network, the EDX map of the cross-section of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs hydrogel was obtained (Fig. 2c). As it is observable from the EDX mapping, the C, O, and Al elements uniformly dispersed in the bio-nanocomposite matrix and the agglomeration has not occurred. The finding of the EDX mapping shows the good dispersion of the MIL-53 within the bio-nanocomposite matrix.

The changes in the morphology of the CMC/GQDs hydrogel could be a good sign for the in situ synthesis of the MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix. A scanning electron microscope was used to investigate the morphology of the prepared compounds and the obtained images are shown in Fig. 3 . SEM analysis showed that the neat MIL-53 has an octahedral morphology. Comparing the surface morphology of the CMC/GQDs with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite exhibited the formation of several new octahedral cubics in the bio-nanocomposite structure, which could be a suitable proof for in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix. As well as, from the SEM image of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite it could found that the MIL-53 are well dispersed in the CMC/GQDs matrix. These results in surface morphology showed the successful formation of the well-dispersed MIL-53 in CMC/GQDs matrix and the SEM results are in good accordance with EDX results.

The porosity of the drug delivery system is one of the main effective parameters that could affect the efficiency of drug delivery systems. Hence, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption test was performed at −196°C to determine the porosity of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite. Based on the BET analysis (Fig. 4a), the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs exhibited as a type II isotherm in the IUPAC classification. Moreover, the surface area, total pore volume, and mean pore diameter obtained about 1.1395 m2/g, 0.0053 cm3/g, and 18.655 nm, respectively. The obtained results show that the designed porous system efficiently could be loaded DOX in its structure; due to the small size of DOX compared to pore size diameter of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs; 1.5 nm [63].

It is expected that the presence of the GQDs within the CMC/GQDs and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs matrixes induce the PL property to them. Therefore, the PL spectra of the GQDs, CMC/GQDs, and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite was determined after excitation under 365 nm of UV light. The obtained PL spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. As it is showable from Fig. 4b, the GQDs displayed an emission peak at 460 nm. The higher PL intensity was obtained for GQDs and then for CMC/GQDs. From Fig. 4b, it is visible that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs has lower PL intensity in comparison with CMC/GQDs. This observation may be related to the in situ synthesis of MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs network. In fact, an increase in the percentage of the non-photoluminescent portion in the sample's composition decreased the PL intensity. The filter effect, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, and partial quenching could be probable reasons for a decrease in PL intensity. Moreover, by comparing the PL results, it is visible that in comparison to the GQDs, the CMC/GQDs and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs showed an increase in width and partial displacement in the wavelength of the emission peak. These observations commonly are because of the intersystem transitions, internal and external transformations, as well as, self-absorbing phenomena [19,33,64].

DOX is one of the most commonly used anticancer drugs in chemotherapy because of its high antitumor activity. Therefore, for evaluation of the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs ability as a drug carrier, each of the carriers was suspended in the DOX solution. After shaking for 24 h in the dark condition, the DOX loading percentage was calculated by using UV–Vis spectroscopy at 480 nm. DOX loading capacity and DOX entrapment efficiency of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs were obtained higher than MIL-53 (Fig. 5a). The observed drug-loaded value for MIL-53 could be attributed to the hydrogen bond formation and the electrostatic attraction between the polar groups of the MIL-53 and DOX. As well as, the π-π interaction between the benzene ring of DOX and the organic ligand of MIL-53 could be another reason for the observed drug-loaded value. However, in the case of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, besides the above-mentioned factors, several other phenomena could be involved; 1) π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic rings of DOX and the conjugated rings of GQDs, 2) Entrapment of DOX within the three-dimensional network of the hydrogel, 3) H-bond formation and electrostatic interaction among the polar groups of the CMC and GQDs with DOX [7,53,65]. Because the DOX loading percentage and cell viability (See the MTT test results) for the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs were obtained higher than MIL-53, we studied drug release behavior and other biological tests only for MIL-53@CMC/GQDs.

The DOX release behavior of the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs was investigated in two buffer solutions with different pH values; pH 5.0, and pH 7.4 that respectively simulated the pH of the tumor and normal physiological environments. The obtained release profiles are presented in Fig. 5b. The DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed an initial burst release for 12 h that continued in a sustained manner to 192 h. The initial burst release may be related to the part of the drug that absorbed onto the surface of the carrier. The more study of the release profile showed that the amount of released DOX at pH 5.0 is more than pH 7.4. The observed differences in the DOX release amount at two different pH can be explained by two reasons which arose from the special structure of the prepared MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. 1) At pH 7.4 the formed hydrogen-bonding interaction between the polar groups of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and DOX is strong which led to the low release of DOX in this pH. 2) In acidic environment π–π stacking interaction between the aromatic network of carrier and drug weaken; therefore, DOX releases increases [66,67].

Drug release kinetics is vital descriptive characteristics of a drug delivery system. Hence, in this work, to investigate the drug release kinetics of DOX from DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, the cumulative drug release data of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs were fitted with four common mathematical models to obtain the most suitable model. The findings of the kinetic study are presented in Table 1 . The calculated value for the regression coefficient (R2) indicates the fitness of experimental results with each model and the highest correlation coefficient (R2) displays the suitable model that the drug release kinetics follows. At pH 7.4 and pH 5.0, the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs exhibited a higher R2 value for the first-order model. Therefore, the obtained results show that the first-order model fitted is well with experimental results in both of the studied conditions. As well as, the results of the drug release kinetics describe that the DOX released at a rate corresponding to the value remaining in the inside of the drug delivery system [68]. Finally, the obtained “n” values in pH 7.4, 37 °C and pH 5.0, 37 °C revealed the non-Fickian behavior of drug release in both studied conditions.

Cell viability assay is required for each newly prepared drug delivery system to avoid the nonselective cytotoxic effects. The in-vitro cytotoxicity of the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs against the MDA-MB 231 cells were evaluated by using the MTT assay. As it is visible from Fig. 6a, cell viability of the studied samples is dose-dependent and viability decreased as concentration increased. MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed low cytotoxicity in comparison with MIL-53. Otherwise, about 85% and 69% of the cells survived after incubation with these carriers at a concentration range of 0–50 μg/mL. The lower cytotoxicity of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite in comparison with the MIL-53 could be because of the existence of the biocompatible CMC and GQDs in its structure. Therefore our results showed that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite is biocompatible (At a concentration range of 0–50 μg/mL) for use in drug delivery applications [69]. The study of the cell viability after incubation of the cells with DOX, DOX@MIL-53, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (Fig. 6b) showed a dose-dependent manner for cytotoxicity against MDA-MB 231 cells, so, the higher concentration of drug led to the more cancer-killing effect. At the equivalent DOX concentration, the cytotoxicity of the free DOX was obtained higher than DOX@MIL-53, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The observed behavior could be due to the encapsulation of DOX into carriers and interactions between the DOX and carriers that resulted in the sustained, continuous, and slow release of the DOX from drug-loaded carriers and could lead to minimize the needing drug loss and side effects. A similar observation was reported by previously published studies [18,70–72]. The observation shows that DOX could be efficiently carried by MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and released in a controllable and sustained manner from it [54,73,74]. In comparison with MDA-MB 231 the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed the lower cytotoxicity against MCF10 cells (Fig. S1). The higher cytotoxicity of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs toward cancer cells may be related to the more releasing of DOX from the carrier and in the pH of the cancer cell. The output of this test could exhibit the potential of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs for the in vitro killing of MDA-MB 231 as a model of the cancer cells. Moreover, the MTT results proved that the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs could be proposed as a hopeful carrier with sustained anticancer drug delivery.

Cell viability assay is required for each newly prepared drug delivery system to avoid the nonselective cytotoxic effects. The in-vitro cytotoxicity of the MIL-53 and MIL-53@CMC/GQDs against the MDA-MB 231 cells were evaluated by using the MTT assay. As it is visible from Fig. 6a, cell viability of the studied samples is dose-dependent and viability decreased as concentration increased. MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed low cytotoxicity in comparison with MIL-53. Otherwise, about 85% and 69% of the cells survived after incubation with these carriers at a concentration range of 0–50 μg/mL. The lower cytotoxicity of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite in comparison with the MIL-53 could be because of the existence of the biocompatible CMC and GQDs in its structure. Therefore our results showed that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite is biocompatible (At a concentration range of 0–50 μg/mL) for use in drug delivery applications [69]. The study of the cell viability after incubation of the cells with DOX, DOX@MIL-53, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (Fig. 6b) showed a dose-dependent manner for cytotoxicity against MDA-MB 231 cells, so, the higher concentration of drug led to the more cancer-killing effect. At the equivalent DOX concentration, the cytotoxicity of the free DOX was obtained higher than DOX@MIL-53, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The observed behavior could be due to the encapsulation of DOX into carriers and interactions between the DOX and carriers that resulted in the sustained, continuous, and slow release of the DOX from drug-loaded carriers and could lead to minimize the needing drug loss and side effects. A similar observation was reported by previously published studies [18,70–72]. The observation shows that DOX could be efficiently carried by MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and released in a controllable and sustained manner from it [54,73,74]. In comparison with MDA-MB 231 the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs displayed the lower cytotoxicity against MCF10 cells (Fig. S1). The higher cytotoxicity of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs toward cancer cells may be related to the more releasing of DOX from the carrier and in the pH of the cancer cell. The output of this test could exhibit the potential of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs for the in vitro killing of MDA-MB 231 as a model of the cancer cells. Moreover, the MTT results proved that the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs could be proposed as a hopeful carrier with sustained anticancer drug delivery.

The morphology of chromatin is the suitable criteria for the selection of healthy, apoptotic, and necrotic cells. The density and nucleus condition of the cells, safety, necrotic, and apoptosis of MDA-MB 231 cells after 48 h treatment with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, DOX, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs bio-nanocomposite investigated by microscopic investigation of DAPI stained cells. The achieved microscopic image of DAPI stained cells after 48 h treatment with each sample are shown in Fig. 7 . From the DAPI staining test, it is understood that the non-treated cells and the treated cells with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs did not show distinguished chromatin degradation and the nucleus have normal morphology. This observation is related to the safety of the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs against the MDA-MB 231 cells and is in good accordance with MTT assay results. However, the study of the obtained microscopic images for the treated cells with DOX and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs shows the more fragmentation of nuclei, condensation of chromatin, and apoptosis. As well as, from Fig. 7 it is found that the cell viability was significantly decreased after incubation with DOX and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The observed phenomena are related to the cancer cell killing capability of the DOX and its delivery to the cells by DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. The obtained results indicated that the MIL-53@CMC is a biocompatible DOX carrier with the cancer cell killing ability.

Commonly the chemotherapeutic agents lead to destroying of the cancer cells through the arrest in its cycle. In this work, the effect of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, DOX, and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs on cell cycle phases progression in MDA-MB 231 cell line was assessed after staining the DNA with PI. Flow cytometry test output with the percentage of the cell population in each phase reported in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8, respectively about 51.90% and 44.77% of treated cells with MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and untreated cells are in the G0-G1 phase. This shows the expected pattern for unceasingly growing cells, and displayed that the MIL-53@CMC/GQDs has no significant effect on the MDA-MD 231cell cycle. However, the results of cell distribution in different cell cycle phases exhibited an arrest in sub-G1 for DOX (48.15%) and DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (35.01%) compared to the non-treated cells (0.44%) that it is corresponded to apoptotic cells and displayed an inhibitory effect on DNA duplication. These findings highlight the cancer cell killing effects of the DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs via DOX releases.

In this work, a green and simple approach was introduced for the in situ synthesis of the MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs matrix to obtain the combined advantages of MOF and CMC/GQDs for the prepared system. FT-IR and XRD analysis verified the successful synthesis of the MIL-53 within the CMC/GQDs hydrogel matrix. The good distribution of the MIL-53 in the CMC/GQDs matrix was observed by using the SEM images and EDX map. The DOX loading capability of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs obtained higher than MIL-53. In-vitro DOX release behavior showed controlled release behavior for DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs, which is well fitted with the first-order kinetic model and the non-Fickian mechanism. The MTT assay results displayed the biocompatibility of MIL-53@CMC/GQDs (Cell viability of 85%). Furthermore, cell cycle analysis results exhibited that the treated cells with DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs lead to Sub-G1 arrest of the MDA-MB 231 cells, which corresponds to apoptotic cells. Fragmentation of the DAPI stained cells as the result of treatment with DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs and a decrease in the cell viability confirmed its cancer treatment ability. These findings indicated that the prepared system could be potentially considered as a hopeful candidate for breast cancer treatment through the sustained delivery of the DOX to the cancer cells.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Viability of MCF10A cells after treatment with different concentration of DOX@MIL-53@CMC/GQDs. Data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3).
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.08.121.

Malihe Pooresmaeil carried out the experiment and wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi. Hassan Namazi supervised the project. All authors; Malihe Pooresmaeil, Hassan Namazi, and Roya Salehi discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by a research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/2708-980701).
